issues.claim_in_outline: Energy FLOWS one-way (the 10% rule) — matter CYCLES; GPP vs NPP, pyramids that invert; carbon and nitrogen cycles.
issues.description: All concepts (one-way energy flow vs. matter cycling, 10% rule, GPP/NPP, invertible pyramids, C and N cycles) are explicitly in the reference.

issues.claim_in_outline: ~10% incorporated into biomass at the next level; ~90% lost as heat (cellular respiration), undigested material, unused parts; food chains rarely exceed 4–5 levels.
issues.description: Matches the reference 10% rule statement, the 90%-loss breakdown, and the 4–5-level limit verbatim in substance.

These specific kcal numbers are NOT in the reference, but they are clean illustrative arithmetic derived directly from the reference 10% rule (10,000→1,000→100→10). Acceptable as derived-from-the-rule. The 'kcal' unit itself is not specified in the reference but is conventional and not a factual claim.
issues.suggested_fix: None needed; numbers are valid derivations of the stated 10% rule.

issues.description: These numbers (50,000→5,000→500) are NOT in the reference but are clean illustrative arithmetic correctly derived from the reference 10% rule. Acceptable as derived-from-the-rule. The arithmetic is internally correct.

issues.claim_in_outline: GPP = total energy fixed; NPP = GPP − producer respiration = energy available to consumers; highest in tropical rainforests, coral reefs, estuaries; lowest in deserts, deep ocean.
issues.description: GPP/NPP definitions and the full biome productivity ranking (high: rainforests, coral reefs, estuaries; low: deserts, deep ocean) are stated verbatim in the reference.

issues.claim_in_outline: Energy pyramid always upright; biomass can invert in aquatic systems (phytoplankton support more zooplankton biomass); numbers can invert (one tree supports many insects).
issues.description: All three pyramid claims, including the phytoplankton/zooplankton and one-tree/many-insects examples, are explicitly in the reference.

Both ppm figures (~280 pre-industrial, >420 in 2024) and the date 2024 ARE present in the reference verbatim, so they are supported. Flagging only to confirm the citation check on these specific numbers/date passed.
issues.suggested_fix: None; figures and date match the reference exactly.

issues.claim_in_outline: Fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas = buried biomass), carbonate rocks (limestone), dissolved ocean CO2; CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 → lower pH.
issues.description: Long-term storage list, the acidification equation, glucose fixation, and respiration release all match the reference.

issues.claim_in_outline: N2 is 78% of atmosphere; 5 steps with Rhizobium, cyanobacteria, Haber-Bosch (fixation), Nitrosomonas then Nitrobacter (nitrification), denitrification by anaerobic bacteria.
issues.description: The 78% figure, all five step names, all named microbes (Rhizobium, cyanobacteria, Nitrosomonas, Nitrobacter), the Haber-Bosch process, NH3/NH4+/NO2-/NO3- chemistry, and assimilation into amino acids/nucleic acids are all in the reference.

issues.claim_in_outline: Apex predators like lions, sharks, and eagles are rare; grain feeds more people than beef.
issues.description: The reference supports the underlying logic (10% rule, ~90% loss per level) but does NOT mention lions, sharks, eagles, the grain-vs-beef example, or the general claim that apex predators are rare. These are illustrative applications not in the source. Plausibly textbook-standard, hence minor.
issues.suggested_fix: Acceptable as pedagogical illustration of the 10% rule, but note these specific examples/organisms are not in the provided reference.

issues.claim_in_outline: Background/diagram motif: sun → leaf → rabbit → fox → mushroom; meadow capturing 50,000 kcal.
issues.description: Specific organisms (rabbit, fox, mushroom, leaf) and the 'meadow' setting are illustrative and not named in the reference (which lists plants, algae, cyanobacteria as producers and bacteria/fungi as decomposers). Decorative/example only; plausibly standard, hence minor.
issues.suggested_fix: Fine as illustration; just confirming these specific organism names are not drawn from the reference.

issues.claim_in_outline: Module 15 preview: biomagnification, Gulf of Mexico dead zone, water & phosphorus cycles, climate change, invasive species, AP productivity investigation.
issues.description: Only 'eutrophication / dead zones like Gulf of Mexico' appears in the reference. Biomagnification, phosphorus/water cycles, climate change, invasive species, and the 'AP productivity investigation' are not in the reference. These are forward-looking course-structure claims, not factual science claims about this module's content, hence minor.
issues.suggested_fix: Acceptable as next-module navigation; note these topics are outside the provided reference scope.

overall_assessment: The outline is highly faithful to the reference. All core factual claims, numbers, formulas, named organisms, the 280→>420 ppm figures, the 2024 date, the 78% N2 figure, the GPP/NPP equation, the biome productivity ranking, and the acidification equation are directly supported. The illustrative 10% kcal arithmetic (10,000→1,000→100→10 and 50,000→5,000→500) is acceptable as correctly derived from the reference's 10% rule. The only unsupported items are pedagogical illustrations and forward-looking course-structure references (lions/sharks/eagles, grain-vs-beef, rabbit/fox/mushroom motif, Module 15 topic previews) — none contradict the reference, all are plausibly textbook-standard, so they are flagged minor/note rather than critical. Verdict: minor.
